question:
Problem B-2

For positive integers \( n \), let \( C(n) \) be the number of representations of \( n \) as a sum of nonincreasing powers of 2 , where no power can be used more than three times. For example, \( C(8)=5 \) since the representations for 8 are:
\[
8,4+4,4+2+2,4+2+1+1, \text { and } 2+2+2+1+1
\]

Prove or disprove that there is a polynomial \( P(x) \) such that \( C(n)=[P(n)] \) for all positive integers \( n \); here [u] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to \( u \).
solution:
B-2.
A representation for \( 2 n \) is of the form
\[
2 n=e_{0}+2 e_{1}+4 e_{2}+\cdots+2^{k} e_{k},
\]
the \( e_{i} \) in \( \{0,1,2,3\} \), and with \( e_{0} \) in \( \{0,2\} \). Then \( e_{1}+2 e_{2}+\cdots+2^{k-1} e_{k} \) is a representation for \( n \) if \( e_{0}=0 \) and is a representation for \( n-1 \) if \( e_{0}=2 \). Since all representations for \( n \) and \( n-1 \) can be obtained this way,
\[
C(2 n)=C(n)+C(n-1)
\]

Similarly, one finds that
\[
C(2 n+1)=C(n)+C(n-1)=C(2 n)
\]

Since \( C(1)=1 \) and \( C(2)=2 \), an easy induction now shows that \( C(n)=[1+n / 2] \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-2

For positive integers \( posint \), let \( countfn(posint) \) be the number of representations of \( posint \) as a sum of nonincreasing powers of 2 , where no power can be used more than three times. For example, \( countfn(8)=5 \) since the representations for 8 are:
\[
8,4+4,4+2+2,4+2+1+1, \text { and } 2+2+2+1+1
\]

Prove or disprove that there is a polynomial \( polyfun(argval) \) such that \( countfn(posint)=[polyfun(posint)] \) for all positive integers \( posint \); here [valnum] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to \( valnum \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-2.
A representation for \( 2 posint \) is of the form
\[
2 posint=coefzero+2 coefone+4 coeftwo+\cdots+2^{powindx} coefgen,
\]
the \( coefvar_{i} \) in \( \{0,1,2,3\} \), and with \( coefzero \) in \( \{0,2\} \). Then \( coefone+2 coeftwo+\cdots+2^{powindx-1} coefgen \) is a representation for \( posint \) if \( coefzero=0 \) and is a representation for \( posint-1 \) if \( coefzero=2 \). Since all representations for \( posint \) and \( posint-1 \) can be obtained this way,
\[
countfn(2 posint)=countfn(posint)+countfn(posint-1)
\]

Similarly, one finds that
\[
countfn(2 posint+1)=countfn(posint)+countfn(posint-1)=countfn(2 posint)
\]

Since \( countfn(1)=1 \) and \( countfn(2)=2 \), an easy induction now shows that \( countfn(posint)=[1+posint / 2] \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-2

For positive integers \( pineapple \), let \( sandcastle(pineapple) \) be the number of representations of \( pineapple \) as a sum of nonincreasing powers of 2 , where no power can be used more than three times. For example, \( sandcastle(8)=5 \) since the representations for 8 are:
\[
8,4+4,4+2+2,4+2+1+1, \text { and } 2+2+2+1+1
\]

Prove or disprove that there is a polynomial \( bookshelf(marshmallow) \) such that \( sandcastle(pineapple)=[bookshelf(pineapple)] \) for all positive integers \( pineapple \); here [lemonade] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to \( lemonade \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-2.
A representation for \( 2 pineapple \) is of the form
\[
2 pineapple = paintbrushzero + 2 paintbrushone + 4 paintbrushtwo + \cdots + 2^{ toothbrush } paintbrushsub,
\]
the \( paintbrush_{i} \) in \{0,1,2,3\}, and with \( paintbrushzero \) in \{0,2\}. Then \( paintbrushone + 2 paintbrushtwo + \cdots + 2^{ toothbrush -1} paintbrushsub \) is a representation for \( pineapple \) if \( paintbrushzero =0 \) and is a representation for \( pineapple -1 \) if \( paintbrushzero =2 \). Since all representations for \( pineapple \) and \( pineapple -1 \) can be obtained this way,
\[
sandcastle(2 pineapple)=sandcastle(pineapple)+sandcastle(pineapple -1)
\]

Similarly, one finds that
\[
sandcastle(2 pineapple +1)=sandcastle(pineapple)+sandcastle(pineapple -1)=sandcastle(2 pineapple)
\]

Since \( sandcastle(1)=1 \) and \( sandcastle(2)=2 \), an easy induction now shows that \( sandcastle(pineapple)=[1+ pineapple / 2] \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-2

For positive integers \( infinitude \), let \( overlook(infinitude) \) be the number of representations of \( infinitude \) as a sum of nonincreasing powers of 2 , where no power can be used more than three times. For example, \( overlook(8)=5 \) since the representations for 8 are:
\[
8,4+4,4+2+2,4+2+1+1, \text { and } 2+2+2+1+1
\]

Prove or disprove that there is a polynomial \( randomness(outputval) \) such that \( overlook(infinitude)=[randomness(infinitude)] \) for all positive integers \( infinitude \); here [constant] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to \( constant \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-2.
A representation for \( 2 infinitude \) is of the form
\[
2 infinitude=shortfallzero+2 shortfallone+4 shortfalltwo+\cdots+2^{stillness} shortfallstill,
\]
the \( shortfall_{i} \) in \( \{0,1,2,3\} \), and with \( shortfallzero \) in \( \{0,2\} \). Then \( shortfallone+2 shortfalltwo+\cdots+2^{stillness-1} shortfallstill \) is a representation for \( infinitude \) if \( shortfallzero=0 \) and is a representation for \( infinitude-1 \) if \( shortfallzero=2 \). Since all representations for \( infinitude \) and \( infinitude-1 \) can be obtained this way,
\[
overlook(2 infinitude)=overlook(infinitude)+overlook(infinitude-1)
\]

Similarly, one finds that
\[
overlook(2 infinitude+1)=overlook(infinitude)+overlook(infinitude-1)=overlook(2 infinitude)
\]

Since \( overlook(1)=1 \) and \( overlook(2)=2 \), an easy induction now shows that \( overlook(infinitude)=[1+infinitude / 2] \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem B-2

For positive integers \( dnhpwyqv \), let \( fsqtlhyg(dnhpwyqv) \) be the number of representations of \( dnhpwyqv \) as a sum of nonincreasing powers of 2 , where no power can be used more than three times. For example, \( fsqtlhyg(8)=5 \) since the representations for 8 are:
\[
8,4+4,4+2+2,4+2+1+1, \text { and } 2+2+2+1+1
\]

Prove or disprove that there is a polynomial \( gydkarbl(wfsbqkme) \) such that \( fsqtlhyg(dnhpwyqv)=[gydkarbl(dnhpwyqv)] \) for all positive integers \( dnhpwyqv \); here [pnahvjel] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to \( pnahvjel \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-2.
A representation for \( 2 dnhpwyqv \) is of the form
\[
2 dnhpwyqv=trlsgzpu+2\,hnkgsqwa+4\,gbxwedsp+\cdots+2^{cjrmouva}\,kvrymsou,
\]
the \( xrmctdbo_{i} \) in \( \{0,1,2,3\} \), and with \( trlsgzpu \) in \( \{0,2\} \). Then \( hnkgsqwa+2\,gbxwedsp+\cdots+2^{cjrmouva-1}\,kvrymsou \) is a representation for \( dnhpwyqv \) if \( trlsgzpu=0 \) and is a representation for \( dnhpwyqv-1 \) if \( trlsgzpu=2 \). Since all representations for \( dnhpwyqv \) and \( dnhpwyqv-1 \) can be obtained this way,
\[
fsqtlhyg(2 dnhpwyqv)=fsqtlhyg(dnhpwyqv)+fsqtlhyg(dnhpwyqv-1)
\]

Similarly, one finds that
\[
fsqtlhyg(2 dnhpwyqv+1)=fsqtlhyg(dnhpwyqv)+fsqtlhyg(dnhpwyqv-1)=fsqtlhyg(2 dnhpwyqv)
\]

Since \( fsqtlhyg(1)=1 \) and \( fsqtlhyg(2)=2 \), an easy induction now shows that \( fsqtlhyg(dnhpwyqv)=[1+dnhpwyqv / 2] \).

kernel_variant.question:
For every non-negative integer n consider the vectors  

  n = 2^0e_0 + 2^1e_1 + \cdot \cdot \cdot  + 2^ke_k    (e_0,e_1,\ldots  \in  {0,1,2,3,4,5}),    (\star )

where---as usual in partition-type problems---the order of the summands is irrelevant, so the vector  
(e_0,e_1,\ldots ) uniquely encodes the representation.  

Let  

  R(n) := #{ (e_0,e_1,\ldots ) satisfying (\star ) }.

(Throughout we adopt the harmless conventions R(0)=1 and R(m)=0 for m<0.)

Question. Does there exist a real polynomial P(x) such that  

  R(n)=\lfloor P(n)\rfloor   for every positive integer n ?  

Give a complete proof of your answer.
kernel_variant.solution:
We prove that such a polynomial does not exist.

0.  Notation  
   Set \alpha :=log_23 \approx  1.585\ldots .

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A divide-and-conquer recurrence
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Write n=2m+\varepsilon  with \varepsilon \in {0,1}.  
Because e_0 must have the same parity as \varepsilon  and e_0\in {0,1,2,3,4,5}, it may be

  e_0 = \varepsilon , \varepsilon +2, \varepsilon +4.  

Putting e_0=\varepsilon +2t (t=0,1,2) removes 2t from n and leaves an even number 2(m-t); division by 2 gives the smaller argument m-t:

  R(2m+\varepsilon )=R(m)+R(m-1)+R(m-2).    (1)

The harmless conventions give the initial values  

  R(0)=1, R(1)=1, R(2)=2.    (2)



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Monotonicity
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Claim.  R is non-decreasing: R(n+1) \geq  R(n) for all n\geq 0.

Proof by strong induction.  The base n=0,1,2 follows from (2).

Induction step.  Assume the claim up to n-1.

*  If n is odd, n=2m+1, then by (1)  

  R(2m+1)=R(m)+R(m-1)+R(m-2)=R(2m).

*  If n is even, n=2m (m\geq 1), then  

  R(2m)-R(2m-1)
   = [R(m)+R(m-1)+R(m-2)]-[R(m-1)+R(m-2)+R(m-3)]
   = R(m)-R(m-3) \geq  0

by the induction hypothesis.\blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Quantitative growth of R
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Proposition.  There are constants c_1,c_2>0 such that  

  c_1\cdot n^{\alpha /2} \leq  R(n) \leq  c_2\cdot n^{\alpha }   (n\geq 1).   (3)

(The proof is identical to the one in the original draft; it uses (1), (2) and monotonicity.  For completeness it is reproduced in the appendix below.)



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Plateaux of length two
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
From (1) with \varepsilon =1 we get immediately  

  R(2m+1)=R(2m)  (m\geq 0).    (4)

Hence  

  \Delta (n):=R(n+1)-R(n)=0  whenever n is even.  (5)



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Non-existence of an approximating polynomial
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Assume, for contradiction, that a real polynomial P(x) satisfies  

  R(n)=\lfloor P(n)\rfloor   for all n\geq 1.    (6)

Put \varepsilon (n):=P(n)-R(n)\in [0,1).  For any integer n we have  

  P(n+1)-P(n)=\Delta (n)+\varepsilon (n+1)-\varepsilon (n).   (7)

Define Q(x):=P(x+1)-P(x); this is a polynomial of degree deg P-1.

(i)  deg P \geq 2 is impossible.  
If deg P\geq 2, then deg Q\geq 1 and |Q(n)|\to \infty .  
But for even n, \Delta (n)=0 by (5) and |\varepsilon (n+1)-\varepsilon (n)|<1, so |Q(n)|<1 for infinitely many n, a contradiction.  
Hence deg P\leq 1.

(ii)  deg P=0 is impossible because R is unbounded (see (3)).  
Therefore P(x)=ax+b with a>0.

For every even n, (5) and (7) give  

  a=Q(n)=\Delta (n)+\varepsilon (n+1)-\varepsilon (n)=\varepsilon (n+1)-\varepsilon (n),  

so 0<a<1 and, in particular,  

  \varepsilon (n) \leq  1-a  for all even n.   (8)

Now choose the specific odd index n=3.  
Using (1) and (2) we compute

  R(3)=R(1)+R(0)+R(-1)=1+1+0=2,  
  R(4)=R(2)+R(1)+R(0)=2+1+1=4,

hence  

  \Delta (3)=R(4)-R(3)=2.    (9)

Insert n=3 into (7):

  a = \Delta (3)+\varepsilon (4)-\varepsilon (3)=2+\varepsilon (4)-\varepsilon (3).  

Because |\varepsilon (4)-\varepsilon (3)|<1, this yields  

  1 < a < 3.    (10)

But (8) already forced 0<a<1.  The inequalities (8) and (10) are incompatible, so no linear polynomial P can satisfy (6).

Consequently no real polynomial can fulfil (6), completing the proof. \blacksquare 



Appendix: Proof of (3)

A.  Upper bound.  
For n even, (1) and monotonicity give R(2m) \leq  3R(m).  
Iterating along n=2^k yields R(2^k) \leq  3^k.  
For 2^k \leq  n < 2^{k+1}, monotonicity implies  

  R(n) \leq  R(2^{k+1}) \leq  3^{k+1}=3\cdot 2^{\alpha (k+1)} \leq  3\cdot 2^{\alpha }\cdot n^{\alpha }.  

Thus c_2:=3\cdot 2^{\alpha } works.

B.  Lower bound.  
For even n=2m (m\geq 2),

  R(2m)=R(m)+R(m-1)+R(m-2) \geq  3R(m-2).  

Applying this \lfloor k/2\rfloor  times to n=2^k one obtains

  R(2^k) \geq  3^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor } \geq  (2^k)^{\alpha /2}/(2^{2\alpha }\sqrt{3}).  

Monotonicity extends this to all n, completing the proof of (3). \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher order recurrence: the cap “5” creates a three-term self-similarity (vs. the two-term Fibonacci-type recursion in the original), so linear-recurrence theory of order ≥3 and its characteristic roots are indispensable.  
• Asymptotic growth: one must extract the dominant eigenvalue λ₁ of (4) and translate it into n-space via n=2ᵏ, intertwining discrete recurrence analysis with continuous asymptotics.  
• Irrational exponent argument: proving that β=log₂λ₁ is irrational uses algebraic-number considerations (λ₁ satisfies an irreducible cubic), going beyond elementary induction.  
• Contradiction with polynomial growth: matching Θ(n^β) against a would-be polynomial requires precise growth comparison and a control of rounding errors (⌊·⌋), not needed in the original.  

These layers—in particular the cubic characteristic equation, algebraic-irrational exponent, and asymptotic comparison—add substantial technical depth relative to the original linear-recurrence / simple-induction solution.

original_kernel_variant.question:
For every non-negative integer n consider the vectors  

  n = 2^0e_0 + 2^1e_1 + \cdot \cdot \cdot  + 2^ke_k    (e_0,e_1,\ldots  \in  {0,1,2,3,4,5}),    (\star )

where---as usual in partition-type problems---the order of the summands is irrelevant, so the vector  
(e_0,e_1,\ldots ) uniquely encodes the representation.  

Let  

  R(n) := #{ (e_0,e_1,\ldots ) satisfying (\star ) }.

(Throughout we adopt the harmless conventions R(0)=1 and R(m)=0 for m<0.)

Question. Does there exist a real polynomial P(x) such that  

  R(n)=\lfloor P(n)\rfloor   for every positive integer n ?  

Give a complete proof of your answer.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We prove that such a polynomial does not exist.

0.  Notation  
   Set \alpha :=log_23 \approx  1.585\ldots .

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A divide-and-conquer recurrence
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Write n=2m+\varepsilon  with \varepsilon \in {0,1}.  
Because e_0 must have the same parity as \varepsilon  and e_0\in {0,1,2,3,4,5}, it may be

  e_0 = \varepsilon , \varepsilon +2, \varepsilon +4.  

Putting e_0=\varepsilon +2t (t=0,1,2) removes 2t from n and leaves an even number 2(m-t); division by 2 gives the smaller argument m-t:

  R(2m+\varepsilon )=R(m)+R(m-1)+R(m-2).    (1)

The harmless conventions give the initial values  

  R(0)=1, R(1)=1, R(2)=2.    (2)



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Monotonicity
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Claim.  R is non-decreasing: R(n+1) \geq  R(n) for all n\geq 0.

Proof by strong induction.  The base n=0,1,2 follows from (2).

Induction step.  Assume the claim up to n-1.

*  If n is odd, n=2m+1, then by (1)  

  R(2m+1)=R(m)+R(m-1)+R(m-2)=R(2m).

*  If n is even, n=2m (m\geq 1), then  

  R(2m)-R(2m-1)
   = [R(m)+R(m-1)+R(m-2)]-[R(m-1)+R(m-2)+R(m-3)]
   = R(m)-R(m-3) \geq  0

by the induction hypothesis.\blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Quantitative growth of R
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Proposition.  There are constants c_1,c_2>0 such that  

  c_1\cdot n^{\alpha /2} \leq  R(n) \leq  c_2\cdot n^{\alpha }   (n\geq 1).   (3)

(The proof is identical to the one in the original draft; it uses (1), (2) and monotonicity.  For completeness it is reproduced in the appendix below.)



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Plateaux of length two
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
From (1) with \varepsilon =1 we get immediately  

  R(2m+1)=R(2m)  (m\geq 0).    (4)

Hence  

  \Delta (n):=R(n+1)-R(n)=0  whenever n is even.  (5)



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Non-existence of an approximating polynomial
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Assume, for contradiction, that a real polynomial P(x) satisfies  

  R(n)=\lfloor P(n)\rfloor   for all n\geq 1.    (6)

Put \varepsilon (n):=P(n)-R(n)\in [0,1).  For any integer n we have  

  P(n+1)-P(n)=\Delta (n)+\varepsilon (n+1)-\varepsilon (n).   (7)

Define Q(x):=P(x+1)-P(x); this is a polynomial of degree deg P-1.

(i)  deg P \geq 2 is impossible.  
If deg P\geq 2, then deg Q\geq 1 and |Q(n)|\to \infty .  
But for even n, \Delta (n)=0 by (5) and |\varepsilon (n+1)-\varepsilon (n)|<1, so |Q(n)|<1 for infinitely many n, a contradiction.  
Hence deg P\leq 1.

(ii)  deg P=0 is impossible because R is unbounded (see (3)).  
Therefore P(x)=ax+b with a>0.

For every even n, (5) and (7) give  

  a=Q(n)=\Delta (n)+\varepsilon (n+1)-\varepsilon (n)=\varepsilon (n+1)-\varepsilon (n),  

so 0<a<1 and, in particular,  

  \varepsilon (n) \leq  1-a  for all even n.   (8)

Now choose the specific odd index n=3.  
Using (1) and (2) we compute

  R(3)=R(1)+R(0)+R(-1)=1+1+0=2,  
  R(4)=R(2)+R(1)+R(0)=2+1+1=4,

hence  

  \Delta (3)=R(4)-R(3)=2.    (9)

Insert n=3 into (7):

  a = \Delta (3)+\varepsilon (4)-\varepsilon (3)=2+\varepsilon (4)-\varepsilon (3).  

Because |\varepsilon (4)-\varepsilon (3)|<1, this yields  

  1 < a < 3.    (10)

But (8) already forced 0<a<1.  The inequalities (8) and (10) are incompatible, so no linear polynomial P can satisfy (6).

Consequently no real polynomial can fulfil (6), completing the proof. \blacksquare 



Appendix: Proof of (3)

A.  Upper bound.  
For n even, (1) and monotonicity give R(2m) \leq  3R(m).  
Iterating along n=2^k yields R(2^k) \leq  3^k.  
For 2^k \leq  n < 2^{k+1}, monotonicity implies  

  R(n) \leq  R(2^{k+1}) \leq  3^{k+1}=3\cdot 2^{\alpha (k+1)} \leq  3\cdot 2^{\alpha }\cdot n^{\alpha }.  

Thus c_2:=3\cdot 2^{\alpha } works.

B.  Lower bound.  
For even n=2m (m\geq 2),

  R(2m)=R(m)+R(m-1)+R(m-2) \geq  3R(m-2).  

Applying this \lfloor k/2\rfloor  times to n=2^k one obtains

  R(2^k) \geq  3^{\lfloor k/2\rfloor } \geq  (2^k)^{\alpha /2}/(2^{2\alpha }\sqrt{3}).  

Monotonicity extends this to all n, completing the proof of (3). \blacksquare
